body.title: Nanny Ogg — the platform-independent scheduler (cron without the OS crontab)
body.summary: Nanny Ogg owns ALL scheduling. The point (Akien, 2026-06-24): by routing every scheduled job through Nanny instead of the OS crontab, the system gets a platform-independent cron — this has to run on Windows, where there is no crontab. So the schedule STORE is Nanny's {condition, action} registry, NOT the OS crontab. The OS crontab (or Ground Loop) contributes at most a single heartbeat that ticks Nanny; it is not where jobs live. Conditions: cron | gate_date | ticket_closed | threshold | external. Actions: dispatch_ticket | fire_consequence | route_world_ticket | post_to_channel. Nanny connects to the home DB (UU_HOME_DB_URL) for gate-date monitoring and queue polling, so as a non-interactive device she needs that env delivered without a login shell (the env-file work, T-uu-env-file-for-noninteractive).

key_concepts.name: platform independence
key_concepts.what: scheduling lives in Nanny so the same schedules run on Linux and Windows — distance from the OS scheduler implementation

key_concepts.name: schedule store is Nanny, not crontab
key_concepts.what: jobs are {condition, action} rows Nanny owns; the OS crontab is not the source of truth

key_concepts.name: heartbeat-driven tick
key_concepts.what: crontab or Ground Loop sends a heartbeat to wake Nanny; which one does not matter — the heartbeat carries no schedule, it just kicks her

key_concepts.name: DB-connecting device
key_concepts.what: device.py reads UU_HOME_DB_URL for gate monitoring / queue polling — needs the non-interactive env file when launched outside an interactive shell

anti_patterns: Adding scheduled jobs as OS crontab lines — that re-couples to the OS scheduler and breaks the Windows path; register them with Nanny instead.
Letting individual devices schedule themselves — scheduling is centralized in Nanny so it stays portable and observable.
Treating a crontab entry as anything more than a heartbeat to kick Nanny.
